name: Colosseum
description: Two thousand years old and still astonishing at street level. The underground hypogeum and third ring are worth the small upgrade ticket — book well in advance.

name: Roman Forum
description: The heart of ancient Rome, included with your Colosseum ticket. Bring a map or a good audio guide — without context it's a beautiful field of columns; with it, the whole city comes alive.

name: Vatican Museums & Sistine Chapel
description: The Sistine Chapel ceiling is the reason people cry in museums — genuinely. Book timed entry months ahead. The Raphael Rooms and the Gallery of Maps are almost as good.

name: St. Peter's Basilica
address: Piazza San Pietro, 00120 Città del Vaticano
description: Climb the dome for a panorama over Rome that's simply unforgettable. The interior — Michelangelo's Pietà, Bernini's baldachin — is as overwhelming as architecture gets.

name: Pantheon
description: Almost 2,000 years old and its unreinforced concrete dome is still the world's largest. The oculus lets in a single column of light that moves across the floor all day.

name: Trevi Fountain
description: Extravagant, theatrical, and impossible to be unmoved by — especially just before dawn when it's quiet. The coin toss tradition fills the fountain with €1.5 million a year.

name: Borghese Gallery
description: Strict two-hour timed entry into one of the great small museums — Bernini's Apollo and Daphne, Caravaggio's Boy with a Basket of Fruit, Canova's Pauline Bonaparte. Unmissable.

name: Trastevere Neighbourhood Walk
description: The medieval quarter across the Tiber — cobblestone alleys, ivy-draped walls, Santa Maria in Trastevere glowing at the centre. Evenings here are as good as Rome gets.

name: Piazza Navona
description: Built on the footprint of Domitian's stadium — three Bernini fountains, Borromini's church on the west side, and the kind of street performers who actually draw a crowd.

name: Castel Sant'Angelo
description: From Hadrian's mausoleum to papal fortress to prison — the rooftop terrace over the Tiber is one of Rome's best evening views, especially at golden hour.

name: Ostia Antica
address: Viale dei Romagnoli, 717, 00121 Ostia Antica RM
description: Rome's ancient port city, 30 minutes by train — walkable ruins of apartment blocks, bakeries, brothels, and mosaic floors. More evocative and far less crowded than Pompeii.

name: Da Enzo al 29
description: A Trastevere classic — cacio e pepe, coda alla vaccinara, tiramisu made in-house. Tables spill out onto the cobblestones. Book a few days ahead or arrive early.

name: Roscioli Salumeria
description: Half wine bar, half deli — the cured meats, aged cheeses, and their cacio e pepe are famous. The wine list is extraordinary. Book dinner ahead; lunch is easier to walk into.

name: Supplì Roma
address: Via di San Francesco a Ripa, 137, 00153 Roma
description: Rome's best street food: fried supplì (rice balls stuffed with mozzarella and ragù), fiori di zucca, and baccalà in the Campo de' Fiori area. Eat standing at the window.

name: La Pergola
description: Rome's only three-Michelin-star restaurant, perched above the city with a view of the Vatican dome. Heinz Beck's menu is one of the finest dining experiences in Italy.

name: Campo de' Fiori Market
description: A morning market in the square where Giordano Bruno was burned for heresy. Fruit, vegetables, flowers, street food — and the bronze statue of Bruno looking wryly over it all.

name: Palatine Hill
description: Included with the Colosseum ticket — the original hill of Rome, now an archaeological park above the Forum. The views over the Circus Maximus and the city are exceptional.

name: Appia Antica Sunday Bike Ride
description: The Appian Way is car-free on Sundays — rent a bike and ride past Roman tomb ruins, catacombs, and open farmland. An oddly rural escape ten minutes from the centre.

name: Aventine Hill & Orange Garden
address: Parco Savello, Via Santa Sabina, 00153 Roma
description: A walled rose garden above the Tiber with a perfectly framed keyhole view through the Knights of Malta gate straight down to St. Peter's dome. A secret Rome shares only with those who look.

name: Galleria Doria Pamphilj
description: A private palace collection that tourists mostly walk past — Velázquez's portrait of Pope Innocent X, Caravaggio's Rest on the Flight into Egypt, all in gilded rooms. Come here instead of the Louvre.

name: Bioparco Rome
description: Rome's zoo inside the Villa Borghese gardens — good for a slower afternoon, especially with kids. The elephant house and the nocturnal animals section are highlights.

name: Ara Pacis Museum
description: Augustus's Altar of Peace from 13 BC, preserved in a sleek Richard Meier pavilion. The carved procession reliefs are as refined as anything Rome produced.

name: Testaccio Market
description: The best food market in Rome — inside a purpose-built hall in the working-class neighbourhood that invented Roman cuisine. Maritozzi, offal sandwiches, fresh pasta to take away.

name: Baths of Caracalla
address: Viale delle Terme di Caracalla, 52, 00153 Roma
description: The scale is staggering — these public baths held 1,600 people at once. The intact mosaic floors, the barrel vaults still intact, the operatic setting: summer concerts here are legendary.

name: Gelato at Fatamorgana
description: Rome's best artisanal gelato — 100% gluten-free and made with unusual flavour combinations: jasmine and honey, basil pine nut, Sicilian pistachio. Trust the house recommendation.
